unit.name: Reinforcement learning fundamentals and Q-learning intuition
unit.description: Introduces reinforcement learning as trial-and-error optimization, contrasts it with supervised learning, and gives intuition for value-based control through Q-functions and a DQN-style Atari example.
unit.summary: Li defines reinforcement learning as repeated interaction with an environment in order to discover actions that yield higher reward through trial and error [ts=1131s] [ts=1161s]. He highlights four reasons RL is harder than supervised learning: stochastic environments, delayed rewards and credit assignment, non-differentiable dynamics, and nonstationarity because actions alter future states [ts=1234s] [ts=1283s] [ts=1340s] [ts=1389s]. He then introduces value-based RL through the Q-function, which estimates expected discounted future reward for taking action a in state s, and shows how an agent picks the action with the highest Q-value [ts=1456s] [ts=1477s] [ts=1492s]. In the Atari example, the state is a stack of four frames and the Q-function is implemented by a CNN that outputs one value per discrete action [ts=1699s] [ts=1713s] [ts=1746s].

key_points.text: Reinforcement learning improves behavior by trial-and-error interaction with the environment.
RL must handle stochastic dynamics where the same action can produce different outcomes and rewards.
Delayed rewards create a difficult credit-assignment problem across long action sequences.
Many RL environments are not differentiable, so learning may rely on large-scale sampling and zero-order gradient estimates.
A Q-function estimates discounted expected future reward for a state-action pair.
In Atari, a CNN can map a stack of four frames to Q-values for discrete actions and choose the action with the largest value.

kp_catalog.name: Reinforcement learning through trial and error
kp_catalog.description: Reinforcement learning trains an agent by repeatedly interacting with an environment and shifting behavior toward actions that produce higher return.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reinforcement learning through trial and error formalizes how reinforcement learning trains an agent by repeatedly interacting with an environment and shifting behavior toward actions that produce higher return, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Why RL is harder than supervised learning
kp_catalog.description: RL must cope with stochastic dynamics, delayed credit assignment, non-differentiable environments, and nonstationarity caused by the agent’s own actions.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because why RL is harder than supervised learning clarifies rL must cope with stochastic dynamics, delayed credit assignment, non-differentiable environments, and nonstationarity caused by the agent’s own actions, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Q-functions and value-based action selection
kp_catalog.description: A Q-function predicts expected discounted future reward for a state-action pair and supports greedy action selection by choosing the highest-value action.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because q-functions and value-based action selection clarifies a Q-function predicts expected discounted future reward for a state-action pair and supports greedy action selection by choosing the highest-value action, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
